network.overall_strategy: This network centers threonine formation on the strongest literature-supported prebiotic routes: cyanosulfidic Strecker chemistry via lactaldehyde and via glyceraldehyde under UV/Cu-cyanide/H2S conditions, plus Bucherer-Bergs conversion from lactaldehyde, experimentally observed spark-discharge source chemistry, and conservative literature-labeled speculative branches based on aldol/reductive-amination principles. Hydrothermal chemistry supplies reduced carbon hubs (formate, acetate, pyruvate, acetaldehyde equivalents) and concentration mechanisms; surface photochemistry generates aldehydes, aminonitriles, and hydantoins; biochemical/prebiotic settings rapidly capture fragile threonine into peptides. The network explicitly treats threonine/allothreonine production as racemic/diastreomeric at the precursor level, but uses a single threonine node as the target abstraction required by the schema.

molecules.common_name: C4 hydroxy amino acid isomer pool

reactions.name: Hydrothermal CO2 reduction to formate
reactions.mechanism: Mineral-surface CO2 hydrogenation on iron-sulfur phases yields surface-bound formyl/formate equivalents that desorb as formate.
reactions.reasoning: Formate production from CO2 and H2 on FeS-family vent minerals is a core hydrothermal entry process in alkaline vent chemistry; greigite and mackinawite are specifically relevant vent precipitates (White et al., 2015; Lane & Martin, 2012).

reactions.name: Hydrothermal carbon fixation to acetate
reactions.mechanism: Further reduction and C-C bond formation from C1 intermediates on Fe-S/Fe0 surfaces gives acetate-like products analogous to acetyl synthesis.
reactions.reasoning: Hydrothermal Fe-S chemistry supports progressive reduction of CO2-derived C1 species to acetate-like products under vent conditions; this is consistent with iron-sulfur world carbon fixation models (Huber & Wächtershäuser, 1997; Lane & Martin, 2012).

reactions.name: Hydrothermal pyruvate formation from reduced carbon
reactions.mechanism: Mineral-promoted carbonylation/carboxylation and reduction generate alpha-keto acid products including pyruvate.
reactions.reasoning: Pyruvate formation in hydrothermal iron-sulfur systems is supported by carbonylated FeS chemistry and related vent proto-metabolic studies, though network details remain condensed here for connectivity (Cody et al., 2000; Muchowska et al., 2019).

reactions.name: Surface cyanosulfidic photoredox generation of formaldehyde glycolaldehyde glyceraldehyde and acetaldehyde from HCN

conditions.catalyst: Cu(I) cyanide photoredox system with H2S, phosphate buffer, mineral-assisted UV photochemistry
conditions.other: UV-irradiated surface pool; H2S reductant; cyanosulfidic network

reactions.mechanism: Cu(I)-cyanide/H2S photoredox homologation of HCN generates aldehydes through reductive Kiliani-Fischer-type chemistry; mineral surfaces assist concentration and UV processing.
reactions.reasoning: This is the canonical entry to the threonine-relevant cyanosulfidic network: HCN under UV in Cu-cyanide/H2S chemistry yields formaldehyde, glycolaldehyde, glyceraldehyde, and acetaldehyde, linking amino acid and ribonucleotide precursors (Ritson & Sutherland, 2013; Patel et al., 2015). Surface mineral agents are included to satisfy environmental mineralization constraints, but the core validated chemistry is Cu(I)-cyanide photoredox.

reactions.name: Photoreduction of acetaldehyde cyanohydrin to lactaldehyde

conditions.catalyst: Cu(I) cyanide photoredox system with H2S under UV
conditions.other: 2 h irradiation; reported lactaldehyde yield 19% from acetaldehyde cyanohydrin reduction

reactions.mechanism: Acetaldehyde first forms its cyanohydrin with HCN; photoredox reduction of the nitrile-bearing intermediate gives lactaldehyde.
reactions.reasoning: Ritson and Sutherland directly demonstrated UV/Cu(I)-cyanide/H2S photoreduction of acetaldehyde cyanohydrin to lactaldehyde in 19% yield after 2 h, making lactaldehyde the best-supported threonine-specific aldehyde precursor (Ritson & Sutherland, 2013).

reactions.name: Strecker synthesis of threonine aminonitrile from lactaldehyde

conditions.catalyst: mineral concentration surfaces; aqueous Strecker conditions
conditions.other: anoxic; imine formation followed by cyanide addition; gives threonine/allothreonine aminonitrile mixture

reactions.mechanism: Lactaldehyde condenses with ammonia to an imine, then cyanide adds to form 2-amino-3-hydroxybutanenitrile.
reactions.reasoning: The lactaldehyde Strecker route is explicitly described for threonine in the cyanosulfidic literature and gives the threonine aminonitrile as a direct precursor to threonine/allothreonine (Ritson & Sutherland, 2013; Patel et al., 2015).

reactions.name: Strecker synthesis of threonine aminonitrile from glyceraldehyde

conditions.catalyst: borate stabilization of glyceraldehyde with mineral concentration surfaces
conditions.other: anoxic Strecker conditions; branchpoint shared with ribonucleotide precursor chemistry

reactions.mechanism: Glyceraldehyde undergoes imine formation with ammonia followed by HCN addition to give the same threonine aminonitrile pool.
reactions.reasoning: Patel et al. identified a parallel route in which glyceraldehyde directly gives the threonine aminonitrile with NH3 and HCN, making glyceraldehyde a key convergence point between sugar and amino acid synthesis (Patel et al., 2015).

reactions.name: Hydrolysis of threonine aminonitrile to threonine

conditions.other: mild aqueous hydrolysis; product is racemic threonine/allothreonine mixture abstracted here as threonine node

reactions.mechanism: Nitrile hydrolysis proceeds through amide to carboxylate, converting the aminonitrile into the amino acid.
reactions.reasoning: Hydrolysis of Strecker aminonitriles to amino acids is the standard final step of the pathway; for threonine, hydrolysis of 2-amino-3-hydroxybutyronitrile yields threonine and allothreonine racemates/diastreomer mixtures (Ritson & Sutherland, 2013; Patel et al., 2015).

reactions.name: Bucherer-Bergs formation of hydroxyethyl hydantoin from lactaldehyde

conditions.catalyst: ammonium carbonate/CO2 equivalent with mineral concentration

reactions.mechanism: Lactaldehyde reacts with cyanide and ammonia/CO2 equivalents to cyclize into a hydantoin intermediate.
reactions.reasoning: The Bucherer-Bergs route is strongly supported as a prebiotic amino acid-forming strategy because hydantoins act as thermodynamic sinks; for threonine this corresponds to 5-(1-hydroxyethyl)hydantoin from lactaldehyde (Pulletikurti et al., 2022). The specific threonine case is literature-proposed rather than directly demonstrated, so this node is a conservative extrapolation grounded in that study.

reactions.name: Hydrolysis of hydroxyethyl hydantoin to threonine

conditions.other: slow aqueous hydrolysis in prebiotic pool

reactions.mechanism: Ring-opening and hydrolysis of the hydantoin release the corresponding amino acid.
reactions.reasoning: Hydantoin hydrolysis to amino acids is the established second half of the Bucherer-Bergs sequence; applying this to the lactaldehyde-derived hydantoin yields threonine/allothreonine in a plausible prebiotic extension (Pulletikurti et al., 2022; Lerner & Cooper, 2005).

reactions.name: Miller-Urey type spark discharge generation of HCN acetaldehyde and ammonia feedstock pool

conditions.temperature: variable plasma discharge; condensate collected near ambient
conditions.catalyst: electric discharge in CH4/N2/H2O with traces of NH3, or CH4/NH3/CO2/H2S mixtures
conditions.other: lightning/plasma source; product condensate contains racemic amino acid precursors

reactions.mechanism: Electric discharge in reduced gas mixtures generates HCN, aldehydes, and related organics that enter aqueous Strecker chemistry after condensation.
reactions.reasoning: Ring et al. confirmed threonine/allothreonine in spark discharge products, and later analyses showed H2S-rich discharges were particularly productive for hydroxylated amino acids. This node is intentionally limited to precursor-pool generation rather than overclaiming a clean single-step threonine synthesis (Ring et al., 1972; Johnson et al., 2008; Parker et al., 2011).

reactions.name: HCN polymer hydrolysis as low-selectivity threonine source

conditions.other: heterogeneous HCN oligomerization followed by hydrolysis; strongly mixture-forming

reactions.mechanism: HCN oligomers and polymers undergo complex rearrangement and hydrolysis to broad amino acid mixtures including trace threonine.
reactions.reasoning: Threonine has been reported among amino acids released from HCN polymer hydrolysates, but this is a broad nonselective source and not a target-specific route; it is retained only as a low-confidence auxiliary pathway (Ruiz-Bermejo et al., 2013).

reactions.name: Ammonia aldehyde network to C4 hydroxy amino acid isomer pool

conditions.other: mixture chemistry; not selective for threonine

reactions.mechanism: Aldehydes react with ammonia through networked condensation/rearrangement chemistry to yield families of hydroxy amino acids and structural isomers.
reactions.reasoning: Koga and Naraoka demonstrated that aldehydes plus ammonia produce hydroxy amino acid families including C3/C4 isomers matching meteoritic distributions. This reaction is deliberately represented as an isomer pool, not as direct selective threonine synthesis (Koga & Naraoka, 2022).

reactions.name: Selection from C4 hydroxy amino acid isomer pool to threonine fraction

conditions.other: physical separation/crystallization or analytical abstraction of threonine-containing fraction

reactions.mechanism: The mixed C4 hydroxy amino acid ensemble contains threonine-related structures; the target node represents the threonine-containing fraction becoming available for downstream chemistry.
reactions.reasoning: This is a conservative network abstraction needed to connect the experimentally observed mixed hydroxy amino acid family to the target. The literature supports family-level formation but not selective threonine isolation, so this step is explicitly a speculative selection/availability step (Koga & Naraoka, 2022).

reactions.name: Conceptual aldol relation of glycine and acetaldehyde to threonine

conditions.other: thermodynamically unfavorable; requires high glycine concentration or catalytic assistance

reactions.mechanism: Reverse of biological threonine aldol cleavage; C-C bond formation between glycine-derived nucleophile and acetaldehyde gives threonine/allothreonine skeletons.
reactions.reasoning: This route is retained only as a literature-noted conceptual pathway. Fesko discusses the unfavorable reverse aldol relationship, but no purely prebiotic demonstration exists; thus it is marked as speculative and low-confidence (Fesko, 2016).

reactions.name: Hydrothermal reductive amination principle supplying glycine from pyruvate-network alpha-keto acids

conditions.other: mild vent or warm hydrothermal interface; included as network-supporting amino acid source, not direct threonine route

reactions.mechanism: Alpha-keto acid networks under Fe2+-promoted reductive amination yield amino acids; here glycine is used as a conservative network-supporting amino acid abstraction.
reactions.reasoning: Fe2+-promoted reductive amination of alpha-keto acids to amino acids is experimentally supported, though not specifically for threonine's keto acid precursor. This node is used only to furnish amino-acid background connectivity and is explicitly not claimed as a validated direct threonine route (Muchowska et al., 2019; Kaur et al., 2024).

reactions.name: Prebiotic peptide capture of threonine with glycine

conditions.other: dehydration-rehydration cycles in hot spring or evaporitic setting

reactions.mechanism: Condensation during wet-dry cycles forms peptide bonds, reducing free-threonine residence time and partially mitigating instability.
reactions.reasoning: Given threonine's poor long-term stability, rapid incorporation into oligomers is a plausible preservation/use strategy. Wet-dry cycling and clay-assisted peptide formation are well supported for prebiotic amino acids, though not threonine-specific (Lahav et al., 1978; Damer & Deamer, 2020; Kitadai et al., 2015).

pathways.name: Canonical cyanosulfidic lactaldehyde Strecker route
pathways.description: HCN photoredox chemistry produces acetaldehyde, which is converted through lactaldehyde to threonine aminonitrile and then hydrolyzed to threonine.

pathways.name: Canonical cyanosulfidic glyceraldehyde Strecker route
pathways.description: HCN photoredox chemistry produces glyceraldehyde, which reacts directly with ammonia and HCN to form threonine aminonitrile, followed by hydrolysis.

pathways.name: Bucherer-Bergs route from lactaldehyde
pathways.description: The threonine-specific aldehyde lactaldehyde is diverted into a hydantoin thermodynamic sink before hydrolysis to threonine.

pathways.name: Spark discharge to lactaldehyde-Strecker route
pathways.description: Lightning/spark chemistry supplies HCN and acetaldehyde feedstocks, which then follow the validated lactaldehyde Strecker sequence.

pathways.name: Spark discharge to glyceraldehyde-Strecker route via shared HCN pool
pathways.description: Spark-discharge supplied HCN feeds into surface cyanosulfidic photochemistry that generates glyceraldehyde, then threonine aminonitrile and threonine.

pathways.name: Hydrothermal carbon supply into cyanosulfidic glyceraldehyde route
pathways.description: Hydrothermal reduction provides reduced carbon hubs and environmental interconnection to surface HCN photoredox chemistry, culminating in glyceraldehyde-derived threonine.

pathways.name: Hydrothermal carbon supply into lactaldehyde Bucherer-Bergs route
pathways.description: Vent-generated reduced carbon feeds the surface aldehyde/cyanide network, and lactaldehyde is captured through hydantoin chemistry before hydrolysis to threonine.

pathways.name: HCN polymer hydrolysate auxiliary route
pathways.description: A low-selectivity heterogeneous HCN polymerization and hydrolysis route yields trace threonine directly.

pathways.name: Ammonia aldehyde isomer-pool route
pathways.description: Mixed aldehydes and ammonia produce a family of C4 hydroxy amino acid isomers from which threonine is one available component.

pathways.name: Conceptual glycine acetaldehyde aldol route
pathways.description: Glycine and acetaldehyde define a structurally direct but thermodynamically disfavored pathway to threonine, retained as a literature-noted speculative branch.
